Coverage for customers' vehicles in the care, custody, or control of an insured garage operation (repair shop, body shop, valet service). Covers damage to customer vehicles from fire, theft, collision, and vandalism while at the insured's premises.

European Union regulation on data protection and privacy. Applies to any organization processing data about EU residents. Includes breach notification requirements, data subject rights, and significant penalties for violations.
The maximum a CGL policy will pay for all covered claims during the policy period, except those under the products/completed operations aggregate. Typically 2x the per-occurrence limit.

Covers bodily injury or property damage caused during business operations. Covers incidents on the business premises, slip-and-fall accidents, damage to customer property, and advertising injury. Sometimes called Commercial General Liability (CGL).

The accounting rules most companies use for general financial reporting. Insurers use GAAP for SEC filings and SAP for state regulators. GAAP is more focused on matching revenue to expenses in the period earned.

25/50/25 — $25,000 bodily injury per person, $50,000 bodily injury per accident, $25,000 property damage per accident. Minimum legal limits; most agents recommend higher (100/300/100 is common) to protect personal assets.
25/50/25 — $25,000 bodily injury per person, $50,000 bodily injury per accident, $25,000 property damage per accident.

A discount offered to drivers who have been licensed for a specified period (often 3+ years), have minimal violations on their record, and have not been found at fault in accidents causing bodily injury. Required in some states.
Acting honestly and fairly, with good intentions and full disclosure.

An insurance program where risks are transferred to a government agency. Also known as social insurance. It usually exists in situations where the exposure to loss is too high for private insurers to reliably insure risks.

A specified period immediately following the premium due date during which a payment can be made to continue a policy in force without interruption. Applies only to life and health policies. Check your policy for specifics.
The total cost of an insurance policy. Formula: Expected Losses + Risk Charge + Administrative Loading.
Ability to write off premiums as a true risk transfer, provided the parent's own risk constitutes no more than 30% of the total risk portfolio.

A state-operated fund that pays claims of insolvent insurers. Funded through assessments on licensed (admitted) insurers. The reason buying from an admitted insurer provides extra consumer protection.
Workers who fell trees with chainsaws rather than mechanized equipment. Highest exposure to struck-by hazards; required to complete state-certified training in many jurisdictions.
A period in the insurance cycle characterized by high demand and low supply — premiums rise, underwriting tightens, coverage becomes harder to place.

term: Hartford Steam Boiler — Equipment Start-Up, Shutdown & Maintenance Fact Sheets
definition: Technical guidance from Hartford Steam Boiler Inspection and Insurance Company on safe operation and maintenance of boilers, pressure vessels, and industrial equipment. Core reference for equipment breakdown loss control.
A condition that increases the likelihood or severity of a loss from a peril. Physical hazards (icy walkway), moral hazards (dishonesty), and morale hazards (carelessness).
Evaluating identified hazards to determine their potential severity and likelihood of causing loss. Prioritizes hazards for treatment based on risk ranking (probability × severity).

OSHA's "right to know" standard requiring employers to inform workers about chemical hazards in the workplace through labels, Safety Data Sheets, and training. Also called the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) after the 2012 update.
Actions taken to eliminate or reduce hazards. Follows the hierarchy of controls: elimination first, then substitution, engineering controls, administrative controls, and PPE last.

The process of finding hazards in the workplace — walk-throughs, employee interviews, incident analysis, job hazard analysis, SDS review, and external references (OSHA standards, industry benchmarks). The first step of any hazard management program.

Condensed reference materials covering specific hazards — active shooter, avalanche, cyberattack, earthquake, extreme heat, financial emergency, flood, hurricane, landslide, pandemic, power outage, thunderstorm, tornado, tsunami, volcano, wildfire, winter storm. Free downloadable sheets are published by FEMA and Ready.gov.

The systematic process of identifying hazards, assessing their likelihood and severity, implementing controls to prevent or mitigate them, and monitoring results over time. Many workplace hazards — accidents, chemical spills, equipment breakdown, fires — can be prevented through this structured approach.
Any legal action that increases the chance of a loss.
Conscious and deliberate action on the part of the insured that increases the chance of a loss. All conscious acts committed with a hope to defraud the insurance company are moral hazards.
Unconscious tendency of insured people to engage in riskier behavior.
Any physical condition that increases the chance of loss.

The amount the insured pays each year before the plan begins paying. After the deductible is met, the plan shares costs through copays or coinsurance until the out-of-pocket maximum is reached. Higher deductibles = lower premiums.

Coverage for medical expenses. May be purchased individually or through group plans (employer-based). Coverage is subject to deductibles, copays, coinsurance, and out-of-pocket maximums. Key coverage categories: hospital, surgical, physician, prescription drug, preventive care, mental health, maternity.

A managed care plan requiring members to use a network of approved providers (except emergencies). Members typically choose a Primary Care Physician (PCP) who coordinates care and refers to specialists. Lowest premiums among managed care options; least provider flexibility.

A tax-advantaged savings account available only to people enrolled in an HDHP. Contributions are tax-deductible; growth is tax-free; withdrawals for qualifying medical expenses are tax-free. Funds roll over year to year. Effectively a triple tax benefit.

The preferred order for controlling workplace hazards, from most to least effective: (1) Elimination — remove the hazard entirely; (2) Substitution — replace with something safer; (3) Engineering controls — isolate people from the hazard; (4) Administrative controls — change how people work; (5) PPE — personal protective equipment as the last line of defense.
A categorization of workplace hazards commonly used in risk assessment: physical, chemical, biological, ergonomic, and psychosocial. Each requires different controls.

A plan with a high deductible (set annually by the IRS — for 2024: at least $1,600 individual / $3,200 family) and lower premium. Qualifies the insured to contribute to a Health Savings Account (HSA).

Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act rules requiring safeguards for electronic protected health information (ePHI). Applies to healthcare providers, health plans, and their business associates.
Health Maintenance Organization. A health insurance plan in which patients receive health care from designated providers.

The most common homeowners policy form. Covers the dwelling on an open-perils (all risk) basis and personal property on a named-perils basis. The "standard" coverage for single-family owner-occupied homes.

The most commonly written homeowners form. Covers the dwelling and other structures on an open-perils basis (all causes of loss except exclusions). Covers personal property on a named-perils basis (16 listed perils). A balanced form suitable for most owner-occupied single-family homes.
Homeowners form for renters. Covers personal property, personal liability, and additional living expenses. Does NOT cover the structure itself, which is the landlord's responsibility.

Designed for renters. Covers personal property, personal liability, medical payments, and additional living expenses. Does NOT cover the structure — that is the landlord's responsibility. Renters insurance is inexpensive and strongly recommended.

Covers both the dwelling AND personal property on an open-perils basis. Broader contents coverage than HO-3 — no need to prove the cause of loss was a named peril. More expensive but recommended for higher-value homes and contents.

A more comprehensive homeowners form that covers BOTH the dwelling AND personal property on an open-perils basis. More expensive than HO-3 but provides stronger contents protection. Often the recommended form for higher-value homes.

Designed for condo owners. Covers interior improvements and betterments (flooring, cabinetry, fixtures inside the unit), personal property, personal liability, and loss assessment from the condo association. The exterior and common areas are covered by the master policy purchased by the association.

Homeowners form for condominium unit owners. Covers interior fixtures and improvements, personal property, personal liability, and loss assessment. The condo association's master policy covers the exterior structure and common areas.

Homeowners form designed for older homes where replacement cost would far exceed market value. Provides "functional replacement" coverage rather than full replacement cost — replaces damaged materials with modern equivalents rather than matching ornate original features.
A type of Homeowner's policy that provides coverage to both Coverage A (Structure) and Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.

A type of homeowner's policy that provides coverage to Coverage A (Structure) for All Risks of Physical Loss, unless specifically excluded, and to Coverage B (Personal Property) on a Named Peril basis.

A contract provision where one party agrees to bear financial responsibility for specified losses to another party. Often combined with an indemnification clause. Some states (e.g., via anti-indemnity statutes) limit enforceability of broad hold-harmless clauses in construction contracts.

An endorsement covering occasional short-term rental (Airbnb, VRBO) of a home. Standard homeowners policies typically exclude commercial use; this endorsement adds coverage for qualifying rental activity.

Coverage for the physical structure of the main home, including attached structures (attached garage, deck). Written at a limit equal to the cost to rebuild. Open-perils on HO-3 and HO-5 (all risks except exclusions); named perils on HO-4 and HO-8.

Coverage for detached structures on the residence premises — detached garages, sheds, fences, gazebos, pools. Written as a percentage of Coverage A (typically 10%). Can be increased by endorsement if the detached structures are valuable.

Coverage for the insured's belongings — furniture, clothing, electronics, appliances. Typically 50-70% of Coverage A, but can be increased. Coverage applies anywhere in the world, though off-premises coverage is usually sub-limited. Named perils on HO-3 and HO-4; open perils on HO-5.

term: Homeowners Coverage D — Loss of Use
definition: Coverage for additional living expenses when the home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss. Pays hotel, rental housing, additional food costs above normal, and other costs needed to maintain the insured's standard of living while the home is being repaired. Typically 20-30% of Coverage A.

Liability coverage for the insured's personal activities and premises. Covers bodily injury and property damage to third parties for which the insured is legally responsible — dog bites, slip-and-fall on property, damage caused away from home, and accidental property damage. Typically $100,000-$500,000.

term: Homeowners Coverage F — Medical Payments to Others
definition: A small-limit coverage (typically $1,000-$5,000) paying medical expenses for guests injured on the premises or by the insured's activities, regardless of fault. A "goodwill" coverage designed to pay minor medical bills to discourage lawsuits.
A form of property insurance, available to property owners, that covers losses to the physical structure as well as to their personal property and protects the insured person from liability claims.

Coverage within the CGL for business liability arising from serving alcohol at occasional events (company parties, client functions) by a business not in the liquor business. Liquor Liability (separate coverage) is required for businesses that manufacture, distribute, sell, or serve alcohol for profit.
A document issued before cutting or welding, listing required precautions: removal of combustibles, fire extinguisher present, fire watch during and 30+ minutes after work. Signed by a supervisor.

Written program controlling cutting, welding, brazing, soldering, and grinding. Requires a written permit, fire watch (30+ minutes after work completion), removal/shielding of combustibles within 35 feet, fire extinguisher present, and supervisor sign-off. Hot work is a leading cause of industrial fires.

A written program controlling cutting, welding, brazing, grinding, and other activities that generate heat or sparks. Includes hot work permits, fire watch, and area preparation. Required to prevent fires — a leading cause of industrial losses.

FMCSA regulations limiting the hours commercial drivers can operate to reduce fatigue-related crashes. Includes the 11-hour driving limit, 14-hour on-duty window, 30-minute break requirement, and 60/70-hour weekly limits.

Federal limits on how long a commercial driver can operate. Key limits: 11 hours of driving after 10 hours off-duty, 14-hour on-duty window after 10 hours off, 30-minute break after 8 hours of driving, 60/70-hour weekly limits (60 in 7 days or 70 in 8 days depending on carrier operation).
The practice of maintaining an orderly, clean workplace. Critical for fire safety (removing combustibles), slip-and-fall prevention, and efficient operations. A common loss-control recommendation.

Insurance is a contract-based mechanism for transferring risk. A policyholder pays a premium to an insurance company in exchange for the insurer's promise to pay for certain losses if they occur. The insurer collects premiums from a large number of policyholders, invests those premiums, and uses the pool of funds to pay claims. Because losses are statistically predictable across large groups even though unpredictable for any one individual, the insurer can charge a premium that reflects the expected cost of claims plus expenses plus a profit margin.
Health Savings Account. An account where you contribute pre-tax dollars for expected medical expenses.
Coverage for damage to a vessel (ship, boat, or aircraft) and affixed items. The marine equivalent of property damage coverage for the vehicle itself.
A felled tree that has not fallen fully to the ground because it was caught on another tree. Extremely dangerous; requires specialized release techniques. Never climbed or worked under.
A compact among U.S. states and Canadian provinces that simplifies fuel tax reporting for motor carriers operating across jurisdictions. Carriers report once and taxes are allocated to states.
The possible effect on the organization if a risk event occurs. Measured in dollars, lost operational capacity, reputation damage, or other terms depending on the risk.
Involves both technical decisions (which controls to install, what coverage to buy) and managerial decisions (budget, staffing, communication).

A component of NIMS that provides a standardized hierarchical structure for managing incidents. Used during emergencies to establish clear chains of command, coordinate resources, and manage communications. Businesses can use ICS to manage disruptions, public information, and crisis communications.
The structured process of examining an accident, injury, or near miss to determine what happened, why it happened, and what should be done to prevent recurrence.

The coordinated process of responding to and managing an incident — from initial detection through recovery. Required for any organization with emergency response teams or interfaces with public emergency services.

A life insurance and annuity provision limiting the time within which the insurer has the legal right to void the contract on grounds of material misrepresentation in the application — usually two years.

Claims that have occurred but the insurer has not yet been notified of them at the reporting date. Insurers maintain reserves for IBNR to fund future claim payments. Critical for accurate financial reporting.
Expenses that have already been sustained and that have not yet been paid. Additional Living Expenses payments are a reimbursement of Incurred Expenses.
Reimbursement for a loss, which leaves the claimant in the same financial position that she was in before the loss.

A provision requiring one party (the indemnitor) to compensate another (the indemnitee) for specified losses. Three common scope levels: limited (indemnitor covers only its own negligence); intermediate (covers indemnitor's and shared negligence); broad (covers indemnitee's sole negligence). Enforceability varies by state and contract type.
To restore by payment, repair, or replacement.
Insurance that indemnifies loss as opposed to liability.

A general legal principle that an individual recovering under an insurance policy should be restored to approximately the same financial position they were in prior to the loss — not profit from the loss.
A contract adjuster retained by the insurer to handle specific claims. Common during catastrophe situations when staff adjuster capacity is overwhelmed. Still represents the insurer.
A representative of multiple insurance companies who sells and services policies. Owns the book of business and operates under the American Agency System.
Extends personal auto coverage to immediate family members of the named insured.

The science of recognizing, evaluating, and controlling occupational health hazards — noise, chemicals, biological agents, ergonomic stressors. Industrial hygienists conduct air sampling, noise monitoring, and ergonomic assessments.

Automatically increases coverage limits over the policy period by a stated percentage to keep pace with inflation. Important because construction costs have risen significantly — static limits leave policyholders underinsured at renewal.
Pays lost business income when physical access to the insured's premises is blocked by a covered cause of loss, even when the premises itself is not damaged.

Coverage for property that may be in transit, held by a bailee, at a fixed location, a movable good often at different locations (e.g., off-road construction equipment), or scheduled property (antiques, fine art). Also covers instrumentalities of transportation and communication such as bridges, tunnels, piers, pipelines, and radio/TV towers.

Coverage for materials, equipment, and fixtures being installed at a job site. Covers in transit, at temporary storage, and at the job site until installation is complete and the item is accepted by the owner.

A right or relationship with the subject matter of the insurance such that the insured can suffer a financial loss from damage to, or destruction of, it. Required for an insurance contract to be legally valid — you cannot insure something you do not have a stake in.
An agreement where you make regular payments to a company and the company promises to pay you money if you suffer a specified injury, illness, loss, etc.
An employee of an insurance company who sells insurance policies and provides customer service.

Professional liability for insurance agents and brokers. Covers failure to procure requested coverage, misquoting, failure to explain exclusions, misrepresentation of coverage. Essential for any agency — E&O claims can exceed an agency's net worth.
The head of a state's department of insurance. Elected in some states, appointed in others. Responsible for enforcing the state's insurance code, licensing, and consumer protection.
Company which sells insurance policies to individuals or to other companies.
An act on the part of either the buyer or seller of an insurance contract in an attempt to gain money illegally.
A contract in which an individual or entity receives financial protection or reimbursement against losses.

term: Insurance — Uncertainties as a Risk Financing Method
definition: Inadequate policy limits, disagreements with insurers over coverage, delays in claim payment, and insurer insolvency.

Financial instruments that transfer insurance risk to capital markets investors. Catastrophe bonds ("cat bonds") are the largest category — bonds that pay normal interest unless a defined catastrophe loss occurs, in which case the principal is paid to the insurer.
The party to an insurance arrangement whom the insurer agrees to indemnify for losses, provide benefits for, or render services.
The insurance company.
An insurer licensed by the state to sell specific lines of insurance within the state (cannot sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Authorized Insurer.
An insurer that adheres to the laws of another country besides the U.S. and its territories.
An insurer that adheres to the laws of a particular state and is housed in that state.
An insurer that has permission to issue policies in a state, but is not housed in that state.
Nonadmitted (or unauthorized) insurers that do not have permission to sell surplus lines insurance.
An insurer not licensed in the state (can sometimes sell surplus lines insurance). Also referred to as an Unauthorized Insurer.

The section where the insurer promises what it will do. For a property policy, it's the promise to pay for damage to covered property from covered perils. For a liability policy, it's the promise to defend the insured and pay damages the insured is legally obligated to pay.
Property that consists of legal rights rather than physical objects, such as securities, trademarks, copyrights, patents, and licenses.

Liability policies exclude losses caused by intentional acts of the insured. Protects the insurance pool from being used to fund deliberate harm. Important in coverage disputes when the insurer alleges intentional misconduct.
Direct financial interest in protecting a unit.

A separate occurrence that "intervenes" between a defendant's actions and damage or loss to another person. An intervening cause may decrease the defendant's liability, if the defendant can show that it was the intervening cause (rather than the defendant himself) that caused the damage or loss.
A reciprocal agreement that allows apportioned registration of commercial vehicles based on miles operated in each jurisdiction. One plate and cab card covers all participating jurisdictions.
A life insurance beneficiary who has a vested interest in the policy proceeds even during the insured's lifetime. The policyholder may only change the beneficiary with the beneficiary's consent.

An advisory organization that develops standard insurance policy forms (such as the Commercial General Liability form), provides rating information, and compiles industry loss statistics. Most U.S. commercial insurers use ISO forms or close variations.

International standard for risk management. Proactive and applicable to a broad range of organizations; maximizes value across a range of organizational stakeholders. Defines risk as the effect of uncertainty on objectives (positive OR negative).

The international standard for risk management. Provides principles, a framework, and a process applicable to any organization regardless of size, industry, or sector. Defines risk as "the effect of uncertainty on objectives" — encompassing both positive and negative outcomes.
"The effect of uncertainty on objectives." This definition embraces both positive (upside) and negative (downside) outcomes.
"Coordinated activities to direct and control an organization with regard to risk."

International standard for information security management systems (ISMS). Provides requirements for establishing, implementing, maintaining, and improving an ISMS. Certification indicates a robust information security program.

term: Job Hazard Analysis (JHA) / Job Safety Analysis (JSA)
definition: A technique that breaks a job down into its basic steps, identifies hazards at each step, and develops controls. Foundational tool in industrial safety and required for many OSHA-regulated operations.
A metric that signals when risk exposure may be increasing. KRIs are forward-looking — they help management spot emerging risks before they cause losses.

A life (and sometimes disability) policy purchased by a business on the life of a person integral to its operations — a founder, top executive, or star salesperson. The business is both the owner and beneficiary.
Coverage for ransom or extortion costs and related expenses when an insured person or employee is kidnapped or the business is extorted.
A risk that is expected and involves little uncertainty — for example, employee injuries at a factory. Managed with routine controls and workers' compensation.
A principle that an insurer will not cover losses the insured knew had already occurred before the policy took effect. Often stated in applications ("Do you know of any circumstance...?").
A risk that is known to exist but whose probability or impact is uncertain — for example, knowing that a hurricane could strike but not knowing when or how severe.

Backward-looking metrics measuring actual loss performance — injury rates, claim counts, lost workdays, OSHA recordable incidents. Useful for measuring results but cannot prevent the losses they measure.
The area where logs are gathered, sorted, and loaded onto trucks. Hazards include struck-by (log movement, equipment), caught-between, and traffic exposure.
Termination of a policy due to failure to pay the required renewal premium.
A principle of insurance: it is more efficient to insure against large, infrequent losses than small, predictable ones. Administrative cost of small claims often exceeds the benefit.

One of the requirements of an insurable risk — insurers need a large pool of similar exposures to accurately predict losses. Example: a life insurer selling only to one age bracket and health class to get reliable statistics.

The statistical principle that makes insurance possible. As the number of similar risk exposures grows, the actual loss experience converges toward the expected (predicted) loss experience. A life insurer with one policyholder cannot reliably predict when that person will die. A life insurer with one million similar policyholders can predict next year's total death claims with high accuracy.

Forward-looking metrics that predict future safety performance — percentage of employees trained, number of safety inspections completed, near-miss reports filed. Allow intervention before losses occur.
The insured cannot take legal action against the insurance company unless the insured has fully complied with all terms of the policy.
One of the four qualifications of a legally binding contract. The purpose of the contract must be legal (e.g., no contracts for illicit drug deals).

A type of compliance risk that occurs when a company breaks the government's rules for companies. Companies facing legal risks may be exposed to expensive lawsuits, criminal penalties, injunctions, or loss of operating licenses. Mitigated through legal counsel, contract review, and proactive compliance programs.
Responsibility to another for one's negligence.
Auto insurance that protects you against medical costs for the bodily injury of others and damages to the property of others if you are at fault in an accident.
Liability insurance indemnifies a third party for damages caused by the insured's negligence.
Legal actions against the insured, including investigation costs, defense costs, and damages (general and special).
Insurance paid to named beneficiaries when the insured person dies.
The limits of an insurance policy represent the highest amount an insurer will pay. Any cost above the limits of insurance is the responsibility of the policyholder.

The total amount the insurer will pay for all losses during the policy period. Often appears preceded by an occurrence limit (e.g., 100,000/1,000,000). The first number indicates the maximum payout for covered losses in any single occurrence; the second number indicates the total amount the insurer will pay for all covered occurrences during the policy term.
Establishes one all-inclusive limit, or maximum payout, for liability damages caused by the policyholder. Contrast with split limits.

An insurance provision that specifies different coverage limits for different parts of a claim. The most common example is split limits in a personal auto policy, which includes three different limits of liability: (1) maximum limit for bodily injury for any one individual, (2) maximum limit for bodily injury to multiple people in any one accident or occurrence, and (3) maximum limit for property damage in any one accident or occurrence.

Coverage for an entity involved in the retail or wholesale sale of alcoholic beverages, or the serving of alcoholic beverages, to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage arising from an intoxicated person. Also called "dram shop" coverage.

Log loaders at landing areas present struck-by and caught-between hazards. Workers on foot must stay outside the swing radius; operators must verify no one is in the path before moving. Hard hats and high-visibility clothing are essential.

The amount that can be borrowed at a specified interest rate from the issuing company by a life insurance policyholder, using the policy's cash value as collateral. If the policyholder dies with the loan outstanding, the amount borrowed plus interest is deducted from the death benefit.
OSHA-required procedure for controlling hazardous energy during equipment maintenance. Workers apply physical locks and warning tags to prevent unexpected startup that could cause injury or death.
A mobile crane or grapple used to load logs onto trucks at the landing. Requires operator certification and daily pre-use inspection.

Specialty motor truck cargo coverage for trucks hauling logs. Addresses unique risks: cargo slip-offs, binder failures, bridge weight overloads, and extended exposures on private forestry roads. Usually written by specialty programs.

A specialty inland marine coverage designed for logging operations. Covers standing timber, felled timber, logs in transit from the forest to the mill, and certain logging equipment. Different from standard property coverage because of the unique exposures of forestry operations.

The high-hazard industry involving felling, bucking, yarding, loading, and hauling timber. Subject to specialized OSHA standard 29 CFR 1910.266 and state-specific logging codes (OR-OSHA, WA L&I, CA Cal/OSHA).

A compliant logging safety program includes: written safety plan, trained and certified fallers and equipment operators, hazard communication, PPE program (hard hats, chaps, boots, eye/ear protection), daily equipment inspection, first aid and rescue plan, weather protocols, and incident investigation.

Narrow forest roads, steep grades, off-highway tire-to-highway tire transitions, load securement, bridge weight limits, and wildlife strikes. Often requires specialty driver training and specific endorsements.

Coverage for services provided in a setting other than an acute care unit of a hospital — nursing home care, home health care, assisted living, or community-based care. Often includes benefits for cognitive impairment or loss of functional capacity.

Replaces a portion of income for extended disability. Benefits typically begin after short-term disability ends (90-180 days) and can continue for years or to retirement age. Own-occupation coverage (can't do your specific job) is broader than any-occupation (can't do any reasonable work).
When someone with insurance endures a negative outcome, such as injury or theft, and expects payment from the insurance company to compensate them.

Costs of investigating and settling claims — adjuster fees, legal defense, expert witness fees, court costs. Allocated LAE (ALAE) is specific to a particular claim; Unallocated LAE (ULAE) is general overhead. Both are factors in pricing.

A homeowners (especially HO-6) provision covering assessments levied by a condo or homeowners association for damage to common areas or liability claims that exceed the association's master policy limits. Typically a small limit ($1,000-$5,000) but can be increased by endorsement.

The practice of identifying, evaluating, and reducing the frequency and/or severity of losses. Performed by insurance company loss control consultants, brokers, and internal risk managers. Includes surveys, recommendations, training, and follow-up audits.

A written report documenting a loss control survey — the risk's operations, existing controls, hazards identified, recommendations, and the consultant's overall risk evaluation. Often used by underwriting to price and accept the account.
An on-site visit by a loss control consultant to observe operations, identify hazards, evaluate controls, and make written recommendations. Results in a loss control report shared with underwriting.

The change over time in estimates of what an insurer will ultimately pay for claims from a given accident period. Newer claims are often underestimated; as time passes, more accurate figures emerge. Actuaries use "loss triangles" to track development.
The chance of financial loss due to some peril. Every loss exposure has three elements: the exposed value (what could be lost), the peril (what could cause the loss), and the financial consequence.

Estimating the frequency and severity of future losses using historical data, trend analysis, industry benchmarks, and stochastic modeling. Essential for retention decisions, budget planning, and insurance buying strategy.

Usually an inverse relationship — as frequency increases, severity typically decreases, and vice versa. High-frequency/low-severity losses are candidates for retention; low-frequency/high-severity losses are candidates for transfer via insurance.

Refers to an insured's history of losses (claims) with other companies, or the company they are currently with. A company will consider "loss history" when underwriting a new policy or considering a renewal of an existing policy. Companies view "loss history" as an indication of an insured's propensity for a claim in the future.

A formal report from a carrier showing all claims an insured has had over a period — typically 3-5 years. Includes dates, descriptions, amounts paid, and reserves. Standard document requested during underwriting and at renewal.

Homeowners coverage for additional living expenses incurred when the home is uninhabitable due to a covered loss — hotel costs, temporary housing, restaurant meals in excess of normal food costs. Typically 20-30% of dwelling coverage.
The party to whom money or insurance proceeds is to be paid in the event of loss, such as the lien holder or an automobile or the mortgagee on real property.
Measures taken BEFORE a loss to reduce the frequency of losses (e.g., fire safety training for kitchen staff before their first shift).
The percentage of premium an insurer pays out in losses and loss adjustment expenses. Formula: Incurred Losses ÷ Earned Premium. A key measure of how well an insurer is underwriting.
Loss ratio = (Losses incurred + LAE) ÷ Earned premium. A loss ratio under 65-70% is generally profitable for personal lines; commercial lines vary by class.
Measures taken to reduce the severity of losses. Pre-loss reduction (e.g., sprinkler systems installed in advance) or post-loss reduction (e.g., quick emergency response).
An amount the insurer sets aside on its books to cover claims that have been reported but not yet paid, plus estimates for IBNR. Critical to measuring financial solvency.
A table showing how claims from each accident year (or reporting period) develop over successive valuation dates. Used by actuaries to project ultimate losses and set reserves.
Physical harm to tangible property caused by a peril.
An economic loss that results from the direct, or physical, loss.

Coverage protecting a licensed health care provider or professional against legal liability resulting from death, injury, or loss to a patient or client caused by the insured's negligence, error, or omission in rendering professional services. Also called Medical Professional Liability.

An umbrella term for coverages protecting managers and the entity from management-related claims: D&O (directors and officers), EPLI (employment practices), Fiduciary Liability (employee benefit plan administration), and Crime.

A provision in a blanket property policy limiting recovery at any one location to a percentage (typically 110-125%) of the reported value at that location — preventing all the blanket limit from going to a single loss.

Coverage for goods in transit and the vehicles of transportation on waterways, land, and air. Split into Ocean Marine (ships, cargo across oceans) and Inland Marine (goods moving over land and scheduled personal property).
In agency operations, to "market" an account is to solicit quotes from multiple carriers to find the best coverage and price. Distinct from direct-writing with a single carrier.

A state regulatory review of an insurer's practices — underwriting, claims handling, advertising, producer oversight, complaint handling. Separate from financial examinations (which focus on solvency). Can result in fines, corrective action orders, and restitution to policyholders.
The price something will sell for in the open market, or "fair market value."

Market value is what a willing buyer would pay for the home; replacement cost is what it would cost to rebuild. These are different numbers — especially for older homes, homes in declining areas, or unique properties. Insurance should be based on replacement cost.
A significant misstatement in an application form. If a company had access to the correct information at the time of application, the company might not have agreed to accept the application.
An authority number issued by FMCSA for for-hire motor carriers transporting federally regulated commodities interstate. Separate from the DOT number.

Federal law enacted in 1945 confirming state regulation of insurance. Exempts the insurance business from most federal anti-trust laws as long as states actively regulate. Foundation of the state-based regulatory system.
The form motor carriers file biennially to update their operational information with FMCSA. Required to maintain active operating authority.
Provides public liability coverage for bodily injury, property damage, and environmental protection. Federally required endorsement for motor carriers.
Property policies generally exclude losses from mechanical or electrical breakdown. Requires Equipment Breakdown (boiler and machinery) coverage.

Coverage for repair or replacement of property that fails due to defects in materials, workmanship, or normal wear and tear. Commonly applied to motor vehicles, mobile equipment, appliances, and electronics.

Logging using feller-bunchers, harvesters, forwarders, and processors — equipment that replaces much of the hand-cutting with enclosed-cab machine operation. Generally safer than hand-cutting but introduces equipment-specific hazards.
Coverage for content-related claims — defamation, copyright infringement, invasion of privacy. Relevant for publishers, broadcasters, content creators, and businesses with active digital marketing.
A federal and state assistance program that pays for health care services for people who cannot afford them.

Auto coverage that pays reasonable expenses incurred for necessary medical and funeral services because of bodily injury caused by an accident and sustained by you or any other person while occupying a covered automobile — regardless of fault.

Homeowners coverage that pays reasonable medical expenses for guests injured on the insured's premises — regardless of fault. Small limit (typically $1,000-$5,000) intended to avoid lawsuits by covering minor medical bills.

A federal assistance program, passed under Title XVIII of the Social Security Amendments of 1965, that provides hospital and medical expense insurance to people over 65 and certain disabled individuals.

Insurance coverage sold on an individual or group basis to help fill the "gaps" in Medicare coverage. Pays part or all of Medicare's deductibles and co-payments and may cover services not covered by Medicare. Cannot duplicate Medicare benefits.
Standardized questionnaires, review of financial statements, flowcharts of operations, and personal inspections of the premises.

An agent or agency with greater authority from an insurance company than a typical agent — may include underwriting, policy issuance, claims handling, and producer appointment authority. Effectively operates as an outsourced underwriting arm.
The lowest amount of auto insurance coverage one can have to be legally allowed to drive in each state.
Any false or misleading statement.

Activities that reduce the severity of an event if it occurs. Different from prevention, which tries to stop the event entirely. Examples: installing sprinklers (fire mitigation), elevating buildings above flood plains, installing surge protection.
Planning of specific actions to reduce the likelihood or impact of identified risks. Occurs after analysis of probability, impact, and speed of onset.
When this endorsement is attached, mobile equipment is considered a covered auto.
Temporary alternative job assignments within the injured worker's medical restrictions while they recover. Enables return-to-work before full release to normal duties.
Many property policies exclude or severely limit mold-related losses. Often subject to a $5,000-$10,000 sub-limit. Can leave insureds exposed to significant remediation costs.
After implementation, results must be monitored and adjusted as exposures change, new risks emerge, or techniques prove ineffective.
Moody's Investors Service's rating of an insurer's claims-paying ability. Ranges from Aaa (highest quality) to C. One of several rating agencies commercial and reinsurance buyers consult.

A hazard arising from the insured's dishonesty or intent. Example: a business owner in financial distress intentionally burning down a warehouse to collect insurance. Controlled through underwriting (declining applicants with suspicious motivations), investigation (scrutinizing suspicious claims), and exclusions (no coverage for intentional acts).

A hazard arising from carelessness or indifference because of insurance. Example: leaving a car unlocked because "the insurance will pay if it's stolen." Different from moral hazard — the insured doesn't want the loss but is less careful. Controlled through deductibles (insured shares the loss) and coinsurance.
Insurance that indemnifies a lender for loss upon foreclosure if a borrower fails to meet required mortgage payments. Also called Private Mortgage Insurance (PMI).
A form of life insurance payable to a third-party lender upon the death of the insured mortgagor. Pays off the remaining mortgage balance.
Requires trucking companies to certify that they are able to meet financial obligations if they become liable for injury or damage arising from their trucking operations.

term: Motor Carrier Act — Limits of Liability
definition: Affects vehicles with a gross weight of 10,000 lbs or more. Required limits are divided into three categories: (1) $750,000 for interstate transportation of non-hazardous property; (2) $5,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of large quantities of certain hazardous materials (compressed gas, radioactive materials, explosives); (3) $1,000,000 for interstate/intrastate transportation of oil or other hazardous materials.
For businesses that transport their own goods and property and may haul the goods of others. Owned passenger autos may be covered autos in the motor carrier coverage form.

Covers commodities being transported by a motor carrier for hire while in the carrier's care, custody, or control. Required by many shippers and by FMCSA for certain commodities. Can be written on all-risk or named-perils basis; common exclusions include loss from temperature failures and improper loading.
Coverage sold to municipalities to guarantee the principal and interest payments on bonds issued — enhances the bond's credit rating.
An insurance company that is owned by its policyholders who participate in dividends. These companies have no shareholders and are not traded publicly.
A parent company organized as a mutual, owning one or more stock insurers. Allows the benefits of both mutual ownership (by policyholders) and stock access to capital markets.
A state-issued driving record showing violations, accidents, license status, and endorsements. Reviewed as part of driver hiring and ongoing fleet management.

Written criteria for hiring and retaining drivers. Typical thresholds: no DUI in 5 years, no more than 2 moving violations in 3 years, no at-fault accidents in 3 years. Stricter standards for heavy trucks and hazmat. Periodic MVR pulls (annual or quarterly) catch post-hire violations.

The U.S. standard-setting body created by the chief insurance regulators of all 50 states, D.C., and 5 U.S. territories. Sets model laws, financial reporting standards, and conducts peer review. Insurance is state-regulated, but the NAIC coordinates standards across states.

term: NAIC — National Association of Insurance Commissioners
definition: The coordinating organization of state insurance regulators. Develops model laws and regulations that states can adopt, operates the Insurance Regulatory Information System (IRIS), maintains national databases, and addresses multi-state issues. Not a federal regulator but influences uniformity.

An endorsement that excludes a specifically named individual from coverage under an auto policy. Common when a household member has a poor driving record that would otherwise make insurance unaffordable or unavailable. Any accident caused by the excluded driver results in no coverage.
The individual, persons, or entities defined as the insured on the declarations page of the policy. The "first named insured" generally has additional rights and responsibilities.
A policy that names all perils that will be covered. Contrasts with open-peril policies.

Named perils (also "specified perils") coverage lists exactly which perils are covered — anything not listed is NOT covered. Open perils (also "all risk" or "special form") coverage covers all perils EXCEPT those specifically excluded — broader coverage. Most commercial property and high-end homeowners are written on open perils.

A standardized approach to incident management established by FEMA. Provides a common framework for agencies and businesses to work together during emergencies. Includes the Incident Command System (ICS).

The rating and statistical organization for workers compensation insurance in most U.S. states. Develops class codes, loss costs, and the experience rating formula that produces a business's Experience Mod (EMR).
An unplanned event that did not result in injury, illness, or damage but had the potential to do so. Reporting and investigating near misses is a key leading indicator.

An evaluation conducted to determine what resources and steps are required to meet program goals. Assess the organization's current state, define program goals and performance objectives, identify gaps, and document the steps needed to close them. The foundation of any preparedness or risk management initiative.
The failure to use that degree of care which an ordinary person of reasonable prudence would use under similar circumstances.

The defendant had a legal duty to act (or not act) in a prescribed manner; the defendant failed to act accordingly (called breach of duty); the plaintiff suffered actual loss or injury due to the defendant's action or inaction; and the loss or injury to the plaintiff was a direct result of the breach of duty of the defendant.
Reduction in net income caused by an event that either decreases revenues, increases expenses, or both. Often covered by business income insurance.

A voluntary framework of cybersecurity best practices developed by the National Institute of Standards and Technology. Built around six functions: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover. Widely adopted across industries.
The central NIST portal for cybersecurity publications, including the Special Publications (800 series), Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS), and NIST Interagency Reports (IRs).

term: NIST — Generally Accepted Principles and Practices for Securing IT Systems (SP 800-14)
definition: The NIST reference on fundamental security principles — defense in depth, least privilege, separation of duties, accountability — that apply to any IT environment.

term: NIST — Information Security Handbook: A Guide for Managers (SP 800-100)
definition: A practical guide for managers responsible for information security programs. Covers governance, risk management, and program management in plain language.
A foundational introduction to computer security concepts for non-specialists. A good starting point for building organizational cyber literacy.

term: NIST — Risk Management Guide for Information Technology Systems (SP 800-30)
definition: The foundational federal document for IT risk assessment. Provides a structured methodology for identifying, analyzing, and managing IT security risk. Referenced by most cybersecurity frameworks.

An insurance company that is NOT licensed in the state where the risk is located. Non-admitted carriers can write "surplus lines" business — coverage unavailable from admitted markets. Not backed by state guaranty funds. Also called "Unauthorized" or "Surplus Lines" carrier.
ISO Construction Class 3. Walls and structural components are non-combustible but may not have extensive fire-resistance ratings (e.g., bare steel-framed building).
Not-for-profit insurance companies that return profits to policyholders by reducing premiums or expanding benefits.
Policy or certificate language stating the insurance will not seek contribution from the certificate holder's own insurance. Paired with "primary wording" in contract requirements.

Auto liability coverage for someone who drives but doesn't own a vehicle — often purchased by drivers who need SR-22 filing, drive rental cars frequently, or borrow vehicles. Does not provide physical damage coverage.

Reinsurer pays only when losses exceed a specified threshold (the attachment point). Includes Excess of Loss Per Risk (per individual loss), Excess of Loss Per Occurrence (per event, often used for catastrophes), and Aggregate Excess (when total losses exceed a threshold).
An insurer's decision not to renew a policy at expiration. Most states require advance written notice (typically 30-60 days). Different from cancellation, which ends a policy mid-term.
An agreement that the insurer asks the claimant to sign which states that the insurer's investigation of the loss can't be used later on as proof that the loss is covered under the policy.

One of the requirements of an insurable risk — the peril cannot cause simultaneous losses to a large portion of the insured pool. This is why private insurers decline to offer standalone flood coverage.
Notice required by insurance companies immediately after an accident or other loss. Part of the standard provisions defining a policyholder's responsibilities after a loss.
The party protected by a surety bond. The person, firm, or corporation for whose benefit the bond is issued.
The party whose performance is guaranteed by a surety bond. Under a bond, strictly speaking, both the principal and the surety are obligors.
A classification of a building based on what's happening inside — office, retail, warehouse, manufacturing, restaurant. Drives both property rates (fire hazard) and liability rates (public exposure).
An event that results in an insured loss or damages.
A liability insurance form that covers claims arising from an incident that occurred during the policy period, regardless of when the claim is reported. Contrasts with a "claims-made" form.

Coverage for goods in transit by ocean vessel, typically written on a "warehouse-to-warehouse" basis. Covers perils of the sea, fire, jettison, and usually extended to include more perils under institute cargo clauses.
Coverage for ocean and inland water transportation exposures: cargo, ships and hulls, earnings, and liability. One of the oldest forms of insurance.

A single insurance program purchased by a project owner that covers all contractors and subcontractors on a construction project. Provides consistent coverage and avoids gaps between multiple contractor policies.

An endorsement or policy option that requires the insurer to pay for Original Equipment Manufacturer parts (rather than aftermarket parts) when repairing a vehicle. Common on newer or leased vehicles.
An open perils policy covers all causes of loss except for those explicitly excluded. Also known as "All Peril."

The risk that day-to-day business activities drain profits or cause losses. Both internal systems (process failures, human error, technology outages, fraud) and external factors (supplier failure, utility outage, weather) can cause operational risks. Controlled through process design, redundancy, training, and business continuity planning.
A deliberate decision to retain a risk, usually because the expected loss is small, predictable, or cheaper to absorb than to insure.

The insurer has three options for settling physical damage claims: (1) pay for, repair, or replace the damaged or stolen property; (2) return stolen property to the insured at the company's expense and pay for any damages resulting from the theft; (3) take the damaged or stolen property at an agreed or appraised value.

An endorsement covering the increased cost to rebuild to current building code. Three coverages: (A) undamaged portion of the building that must be demolished; (B) demolition costs; (C) increased cost of construction to meet current code. Critical for older buildings in jurisdictions with aggressive code updates.

The record of workplace injuries and illnesses that most employers with more than 10 employees must maintain. Includes a 300 log (detailed record), 300A annual summary (posted Feb 1 – Apr 30), and 301 incident reports.

An injury or illness that must be recorded on the OSHA 300 log. Generally includes cases involving medical treatment beyond first aid, lost time, restricted work, loss of consciousness, or significant diagnosed conditions.

term: OSHA — Protecting Workers from Heat Illness
definition: OSHA guidance on heat stress prevention — hydration, rest breaks, shade, acclimatization, emergency response. Increasingly important for outdoor and industrial operations.

term: OSHA — Survey Your Workplace for Additional Hazards
definition: Part of OSHA's Compliance Assistance Quick Start for General Industry. A structured walkthrough for identifying common workplace hazards beyond the obvious. Useful as a loss control baseline for any commercial account.
States that the business auto coverage form provides primary coverage for covered autos owned by the insured; the coverage is excess for losses involving non-owned covered autos.
Homeowners coverage for detached structures on the property — fences, sheds, detached garages, gazebos. Typically a separate sub-limit, often 10% of the dwelling limit.
Modify the policy's liability coverage to meet other states' financial responsibility requirements (minimum limits of liability) when the covered auto is being driven in that state.
The most the insured will pay in a policy year for covered in-network services. Once reached, the plan pays 100% of covered services for the rest of the year. Does not include premiums.
A declaration that a vehicle or driver cannot operate until violations are corrected. Issued during roadside inspections or compliance reviews for serious safety defects.
The amount of money dedicated to a general contractor's cost of doing business and earning a profit. Insurance industry standard is 10% and 10%.

A single insurance program purchased by a project owner covering all enrolled contractors and subcontractors on a construction project. Provides consistent coverage terms, eliminates gaps between contractor policies, and centralizes claims handling.
A credit applied to a commercial package policy reflecting the combined account approach. Acknowledges that packaging multiple coverages with one carrier reduces administration cost.

Two or more distinct coverages combined into a single contract — for example, a Homeowners policy combines property, personal property, and liability coverages. Typically cheaper than buying each coverage separately.

A documented plan for maintaining operations and protecting employees during widespread disease outbreaks. Includes communication protocols, remote work capability, PPE supplies, cleaning procedures, and workforce continuity.

Every policy contains the same core structure: Declarations (who, what, how much, when), Insuring Agreement (the insurer's promise), Exclusions (what isn't covered), Conditions (what the insured must do), and Definitions (specific word meanings). Knowing this structure makes reading any policy faster.

term: PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard)
definition: A proprietary information security standard for organizations that handle credit card data. Includes requirements for network security, cardholder data protection, vulnerability management, and access control.
The maximum a liability policy will pay for any single claim or accident. Contrasts with the aggregate limit, which is the most the policy will pay for all claims during the policy period.
The direct cause of loss — fire, windstorm, theft, collision. Sometimes confused with "hazard."

A peril is the actual cause of loss (fire, theft, wind, collision). A hazard is a condition that increases the likelihood or severity of a peril causing loss. Fire is a peril; oily rags are a hazard. Policies cover perils; loss control addresses hazards.
These are the specific causes of loss that are covered under the policy.

Coverage designed to insure private passenger automobiles and certain types of trucks owned by an individual or married couple. Includes liability, medical payments, uninsured/underinsured motorist, comprehensive, and collision coverages.

term: Personal Auto Policy Part A — Liability
definition: Pays damages the insured is legally responsible for after an auto accident: bodily injury to others and property damage to others. Includes defense costs for lawsuits. Does not cover damage to the insured's own vehicle or injuries to the insured. Required by state financial responsibility laws.

term: Personal Auto Policy Part B — Medical Payments
definition: Pays medical and funeral expenses for the insured, family members, and passengers injured in a covered auto, regardless of fault. A small-limit coverage (typically $1,000-$10,000 per person) designed to cover immediate medical costs without a lengthy fault determination.

term: Personal Auto Policy Part C — Uninsured Motorists
definition: Pays bodily injury damages (and property damage in some states) caused by a motorist who has no insurance, has insufficient insurance, or is a hit-and-run driver. Some states offer stacked UM coverage (combining limits across multiple vehicles).

term: Personal Auto Policy Part D — Coverage for Damage to Your Auto
definition: Covers damage to the insured's own vehicle. Split into Collision (impact with another vehicle or object) and Other Than Collision / Comprehensive (fire, theft, hail, vandalism, flood, falling objects, animal strikes). Each has its own deductible.

term: Personal Auto Policy Part E — Duties After an Accident or Loss
definition: The policyholder's obligations after an accident: notify the insurer promptly, cooperate with the investigation, forward legal documents, provide proof of loss, submit to an examination under oath if requested, and protect the vehicle from further damage. Failure can void coverage.

term: Personal Auto Policy Part F — General Provisions
definition: The general rules of the contract: policy territory (US and Canada typical), termination and cancellation procedures, transfer of interest on the insured's death, fraud consequences, and the insurer's right of subrogation.

Credit insurance that pays the difference between the outstanding balance owed on a vehicle loan and the actual cash value of the vehicle in the event of a total loss. Prevents the borrower from owing money on a car they no longer own.

Liability coverage for claims arising from discrimination, false arrest, illegal detention, libel, slander, malicious prosecution, mental anguish, identity theft, alienation of affections, or violation of privacy. Broader than "bodily injury."

Auto insurance that protects you against the cost of your own medical bills and, in some states, lost wages and replacement services if you are involved in a car accident, regardless of fault. Required in no-fault states.
First-hand site visits to assess risk exposure, observe operations, and identify hazards that may not appear in documents.

Homeowners coverage for claims by third parties for bodily injury or property damage for which the insured is legally responsible — dog bites, slip and fall on property, damage caused by household members away from home. Typically $100,000-$500,000 limit.

Homeowners coverage for contents — furniture, clothing, electronics, appliances. Typically written as a percentage of the dwelling coverage (often 50-70%). May be written on replacement cost or actual cash value basis.

Equipment worn by workers to minimize exposure to hazards — hard hats, safety glasses, hearing protection, gloves, respirators, high-visibility vests, fall protection harnesses. Required by OSHA when engineering and administrative controls are insufficient.

Extra liability coverage above the underlying auto and homeowners policies. Typical limits $1M-$5M. Responds when the underlying limit is exhausted OR for certain losses not covered by the underlying policy. Requires underlying coverage at specified minimum limits (usually $250K/$500K auto and $300K homeowners).
The financial exposure created when key personnel die, quit, retire, or become disabled. Addressed through succession planning and key person insurance.
A veterinary care insurance policy providing coverage for a pet animal (dog, cat, etc.) in the event of illness or accident. May cover routine care, accidents, or both.
Contains three principal coverages: (1) Collision, (2) Comprehensive, (3) Specified Causes of Loss.
Coverage for damage to a trailer in the insured's possession under a trailer interchange agreement. Common in trucking where carriers exchange trailers to maintain efficient operations.

Excluded: sound reproducing and receiving equipment, tapes, and records; wear and tear, freezing, mechanical or electrical breakdown, or road damage to tires; covered autos while being used for organized or professional racing, demolition, and stunting activities.

A tangible condition that increases the probability of a loss — icy sidewalks, unguarded machinery, oily rags near a heat source, faulty wiring, overgrown brush near a building. The primary focus of loss control programs.
A hybrid of HMO and PPO. Requires a PCP and referrals for specialists (HMO-like), but allows out-of-network coverage at higher cost (PPO-like).
Contract between an insurance company and policyholder that explains the amounts and types of coverage.

term: Policy Administration System / Agency Management System (AMS)
definition: The software system an agency uses to manage policies, customer records, billing, claims, and workflow. Major systems include Applied Epic, Applied TAM, Vertafore AMS360, and Vertafore Sagitta.

A loan against a permanent life insurance policy, using the cash value as collateral. Interest is charged but the loan does not have to be repaid; unpaid loan balance is deducted from the death benefit. Tax-free while the policy remains in force.
The period a policy is in force, from the beginning or effective date to the expiration date.

The geographic area where a policy provides coverage. Common definitions include "United States and Canada," "Worldwide," or named countries. Significant for cargo, travel, and international operations.
The individual or firm buying the policy.

Any solid, liquid, gaseous, or thermal irritant or contaminant including smoke, vapor, soot, fumes, acids, alkalis, chemicals, and waste. Waste includes material to be recycled, reconditioned, or reclaimed.

Standard Commercial General Liability policies exclude pollution liability (with limited exceptions). Even minor chemical operations (pool chlorine, HVAC refrigerants) can create uncovered exposures. Pollution coverage often purchased separately.

Coverage for third-party bodily injury, property damage, and cleanup costs from pollution incidents. Most general liability policies exclude pollution; a separate policy is required for operations with environmental exposure.

Written procedures for post-accident actions: driver safety, scene documentation, law enforcement report, injury care, post-accident drug/alcohol test (required by FMCSA within 8 hours for alcohol, 32 hours for drugs for certain accidents), carrier notification.
Authority given to one person or corporation to act for and obligate another, to the extent laid down in the instrument creating the power.

Preferred Provider Organization. A health insurance plan in which providers agree to deliver services for discount fees; patients can go to any provider, but using nonparticipating providers results in higher costs to the patient.

A two-stage system combining features of wet and dry. Water is held back by a valve that requires both heat detection AND a sprinkler head activation. Used where accidental water discharge would be catastrophic — data centers, archives, museums.

Daily inspection required by FMCSA. Driver inspects brakes, lights, tires, mirrors, load securement, fluids, and other safety items. Documented on Driver Vehicle Inspection Report (DVIR). Any defects must be repaired before next trip if they affect safety.

A managed care plan that provides financial incentives (lower deductibles, lower coinsurance) for using in-network providers but also covers out-of-network care at a higher cost. No PCP or referrals typically required. More flexible than HMO.

Coverage for liability of an insured to persons who have incurred bodily injury or property damage on an insured's premises during normal operations, routine maintenance, or from the insured's business operations either on or off the premises. Core coverage in a CGL policy.

CGL coverage for liability arising from the insured's business premises (slip-and-fall) and day-to-day operations away from the premises (damage to a customer's property during a service call). The baseline exposure for most commercial accounts.

Pollution liability coverage for sudden and accidental releases on or from the insured's premises. Standard CGL policies have pollution exclusions; premises pollution coverage is often needed by businesses with even minor chemical use (HVAC coolants, cleaning supplies).
The amount paid by an insured to an insurance company to obtain or maintain an insurance policy.

An annual review by the insurer at policy end to determine actual payroll by class code. Initial premium is based on estimated payroll; audit determines actual and results in additional premium or return premium. Audit noncompliance can result in substantial penalties.

Premium = Rate × Exposure units. Rate is expressed per unit of exposure (per $100 of payroll for workers comp, per $1,000 of building value for property, per vehicle for auto). Modifiers (schedule credits/debits, experience mod, package discount, loss control credits) adjust base premium up or down.
Activities that ensure an organization can respond effectively when an event occurs — planning, training, drills, supplies, communication systems. Closes the gap between prevention and response.
Activities designed to stop an event from occurring. The most effective level of risk treatment — eliminating hazards entirely or reducing their likelihood to near zero.

A structured organizational program focused on stopping losses before they occur. Includes hazard identification, controls, training, monitoring, and continuous improvement. The most cost-effective approach to risk treatment.

A policy provision (and certificate requirement) that the insurance will respond before and without seeking contribution from the certificate holder's own insurance. Common contract requirement when one party is an additional insured.
Minimizing loss — reducing the financial impact of uncertainty on an organization through identification, assessment, and treatment of risk exposures.
Coverage that takes precedence when more than one policy covers the same loss. Primary pays first; excess or umbrella policies kick in after primary limits are exhausted.

Policy or certificate language stating the insurance responds first, before any other applicable insurance. Required in many commercial contracts to ensure the designated party's coverage is not depleted first.

A state rate regulation requiring insurers to obtain explicit approval from the insurance department before using a new rate or form. Contrasts with "file-and-use" states where insurers can use rates upon filing.
The chance of something happening or not happening within a certain number of occurrences.
An industry term for a licensed insurance salesperson — often used to distinguish between agents (carrier-appointed) and brokers (representing buyers). Legal licensing category in many states.
The contract between an agent and an insurance company defining terms of the relationship — authorities granted, commission schedule, termination rights, and ownership of expirations.
The license issued by a state that permits an individual or agency to sell, solicit, or negotiate insurance. Requires pre-licensing education, passing an exam, and continuing education to maintain.

Coverage protecting a manufacturer, distributor, seller, or lessor of a product against legal liability resulting from a defective condition causing personal injury or damage to any individual or entity.
Coverage for the costs of recalling a defective product — transportation, storage, disposal, and customer communications. Often excluded from general liability; purchased separately by manufacturers.

CGL coverage for liability arising from the insured's products after they leave the insured's control or completed work after it is turned over to the customer. Critical for manufacturers, contractors, restaurants, and any business that creates products or performs work for others. Has its own aggregate limit.
A separate aggregate limit for claims arising from the insured's products or completed work. Typically equals the general aggregate limit. Important for manufacturers and contractors.

Protects professionals (designers, architects, engineers, consultants, insurance agents) against claims of negligence, errors, or omissions in their professional services. Essential for anyone providing advice or specialized services for a fee.
Coverage for architects, engineers, and surveyors against claims of professional negligence in their designs, plans, and specifications.

CGL policies exclude claims arising from professional services — requires Errors & Omissions (professional liability) coverage. Applies to architects, consultants, accountants, medical professionals, real estate agents, insurance agents.

A sworn statement by the insured detailing the loss — what was damaged, when, cause, and amount claimed. Required by most policies within a specified time (60-180 days). Delay or failure can affect recovery.
Damage to another person's property. The purpose of liability insurance is to cover property damage to a third party resulting from the negligent or unintentional acts of the insured.

Reinsurer takes a percentage of every premium and pays the same percentage of every loss. Includes Quota Share (fixed percentage of every risk) and Surplus Share (percentage of amounts above the cedent's retention).
Earned premium = (Days coverage provided ÷ Days in policy period) × Total premium. Example: $1,200 annual policy canceled after 60 days → earned premium = (60 ÷ 365) × $1,200 = $197. Refund = $1,003.
A broad form of marine legal liability insurance coverage. Covers third-party injuries and damages arising from vessel operations.

A 1-10 classification assigned to a community's fire protection capability. Class 1 is best (excellent fire department, hydrants, water supply); Class 10 means little or no recognized fire protection. Directly impacts property insurance rates.

A commercial property provision where coverage depends on the insured maintaining specified safety systems (sprinkler system active, burglar alarm functional, central station monitoring). If the system is disabled without notifying the insurer, coverage for related losses can be voided.
The clauses that make up the contract language of an insurance policy — define rights, duties, conditions, and limitations of both insurer and insured.

The direct, dominant cause that sets into motion a chain of events resulting in a loss, without the intervention of any other independent or unrelated source. Determines whether a loss is covered under a policy.

A legal principle determining which cause of a loss triggers coverage when multiple causes are involved. The proximate cause is the direct, dominant cause that sets into motion an unbroken chain of events leading to the loss. If the proximate cause is a covered peril, the loss is generally covered even if later causes are excluded.

An adjuster retained by the policyholder (not the insurer) to advocate for the insured during a claim. Licensed separately in most states. Typically compensated as a percentage of the settlement. Useful for complex or large losses.
The portion of a premium equal to expected losses, without any loading for insurance company expenses, premium taxes, contingencies, or profit margin.
A situation including possibility of loss or no loss, but no possibility of gain. Only pure risks are insurable — speculative risks (stock market gains/losses) are not.
A proportional reinsurance arrangement where the reinsurer takes a fixed percentage of every risk written by the primary insurer. Simple and predictable; provides broad capacity relief.
An estimate of the cost of insurance based on information supplied by the applicant. A quote is not a binding contract — it is subject to underwriting approval.
A price estimate for coverage. A firm quote is subject to underwriting and terms; an "indication" is preliminary pricing given before full underwriting has been done.
The price per unit of insurance exposure. Premium = Rate × Exposure units. For example, $0.50 per square foot × 2,500 sq. ft. = $1,250 annual premium.

A formal submission to a state insurance department seeking approval (or providing notice) of proposed rates, rules, or forms. States use one of several approval methods: prior approval, file-and-use, use-and-file, or flex rating.

State oversight of insurance rates. Three common approaches: Prior Approval (rates must be approved before use), File and Use (rates may be used once filed), Use and File (rates may be used before filing with subsequent review). Most states have modernized toward file-and-use or flex rating.

The process of calculating how much premium to charge for a type of insurance coverage. Considers expected losses, expenses, profit margin, and investment returns. Regulated by state insurance departments.
An organization that collects loss data from member insurers and develops loss costs and advisory rates used in pricing. ISO (property-casualty) and NCCI (workers compensation) are the largest.
A postcard-style reference listing 12 key preparedness actions. Signed up for alerts, documents safeguarded, plans made, supplies ready, etc. Easy to distribute to employees and clients.

A comprehensive FEMA/Ready.gov guide on preparing for disasters within families and communities. Covers general preparedness, specific hazards, and recovery. A common starting point for personal and small-business preparedness programs.
A refund or offering of any part of the agent's commission to the insured as an inducement to buy. Illegal in most states because it discriminates between policyholders.

The process of restoring operations and conditions to normal (or better than normal) after an incident. Includes business continuity, claims management, rebuilding, employee support, and lessons learned.
The maximum acceptable amount of data loss measured in time — e.g., "we can afford to lose no more than 4 hours of data." Drives the frequency of backups and replication.
The target time within which a critical system or process must be restored after a disruption. Drives the design of recovery strategies and tools.
A submission that exceeds an underwriter's individual authority and must be referred to a senior underwriter or home office. Common for larger accounts or unusual risks.
Restoring a lapsed policy to full force and effect. Some insurers require evidence of insurability and payment of past-due premiums plus interest.

A transaction between a primary insurer and another licensed insurer (the reinsurer) where the reinsurer agrees to cover all or part of the losses of the primary insurer in exchange for a premium. Allows insurers to spread their own risk.

Insurance for insurers. A primary insurer (the cedent) transfers part of its risk to a reinsurer in exchange for a share of the premium. Allows insurers to manage accumulation, write larger policies than their capital would otherwise support, and smooth loss experience over time.
The insurance company that assumes reinsurance risk from another insurer (the ceding company).
Commission paid on policy renewals, typically at a lower rate than the new-business (first-year) commission in life insurance; generally the same rate in property-casualty.
Auto coverage paying for a rental car while your vehicle is being repaired after a covered loss. Typically subject to a daily dollar limit (e.g., $30/day) and a total limit (e.g., $900).

A form of property insurance available to renters that covers losses to their personal property and protects them from liability claims. Does not cover the physical structure — that is the landlord's responsibility.
The cost associated with replacing property at current market prices.

An endorsement that converts Coverage C from actual cash value (depreciated value) to replacement cost. Pays the current cost of similar new property without deduction for age or wear. Strongly recommended — a standard ACV settlement can leave the insured with a fraction of what was lost.
